title: Riverpod
objectives: Understand Riverpod via a real-life analogy
Handle `AsyncValue<T>` for loading / data / error states
Test Riverpod state without pumping widgets

sections.markdown:
**Think of Riverpod as a smart utility grid running underneath your whole app.**

- Each utility source is a **provider** — a top-level `final` variable declaring 'here's how to build a fresh Cart / User / Theme / Api client'. It's like plugging a specific appliance into a wall socket.
- Widgets don't need to know WHERE the socket is or which floor of the building they're on (no `BuildContext` walking). They just tap a **`ref`** — a room-agnostic tool — and say `ref.watch(cartProvider)`. The grid delivers the current value AND notifies them when it changes.
- Providers can depend on other providers — `apiClientProvider` reads `authTokenProvider` — and Riverpod tracks the graph, invalidates downstream when upstream changes, and disposes providers that no one is using anymore.
- **`AsyncValue<T>`** is a sealed wrapper — every async provider yields `loading | data(T) | error`. UIs use `state.when(loading: …, data: …, error: …)` and the compiler forces you to handle every case. No more ad-hoc `bool _loading, String? _error` flags.

**Riverpod is Provider's spiritual successor** — same InheritedWidget-style dependency graph, but WITHOUT `BuildContext`, WITH compile-time safety, and WITH `AsyncValue` baked in. In 2026 it's the modern default for new Flutter apps.

**Riverpod** is a compile-safe, reactive state-management + DI framework by Provider's author. Providers are top-level `final` variables declaring how to build a value. Consumers use `ref.watch` / `ref.read` / `ref.listen` — no `BuildContext` required. Common families: **`Provider<T>`** (pure DI), **`NotifierProvider<N, T>`** (sync mutable state), **`AsyncNotifierProvider<N, T>`** (async state with `AsyncValue<T>`), **`FutureProvider<T>`** / **`StreamProvider<T>`** (async reads), **`.family`** / **`.autoDispose`** modifiers.
## The provider family

rows: Async state you also mutate (user profile fetch + update)

## `ref.watch` vs `ref.read` vs `ref.listen`

No — access the underlying Notifier for mutation

sections.markdown: **The rule matches Provider's:** `watch` in `build`, `read` in callbacks. Riverpod adds `.select()` for narrow rebuilds and `ref.listen()` for one-shot side effects — same job as `BlocListener`.
## Common beginner mistakes
**Mistake 1: `ref.watch` inside a callback.** Only use `watch` in `build()`. Use `read` for one-shot access in `onPressed`.
**Mistake 2: Missing `ProviderScope` at the root.** Wrap `MaterialApp` in `ProviderScope`; without it, `ref` finds nothing.
**Mistake 3: Storing a `Ref` past its lifetime.** In `AsyncNotifier`, `ref` is only valid while the notifier is mounted. Don't stash it in an isolate or send it across a channel.

**Mistake 4: Not using `.autoDispose` for scoped state.** Without `autoDispose`, providers live for the whole app lifetime — even when no widget uses them. For per-screen state, add it: `NotifierProvider.autoDispose<...>`.
**Mistake 5: `state.value` on `AsyncValue` without checking.** `state.value` is null while loading. Prefer `state.when(...)` which the compiler enforces.
## Best practices
**1. Always `ref.watch` in `build`, `ref.read` in callbacks.**
**2. Use `AsyncValue.when` — never `.value` blindly.** The compiler forces exhaustive handling.
**3. Providers for repositories, not widgets.** DI shape: `apiProvider`, `userRepoProvider`, then a `NotifierProvider` that uses them.
**4. `.autoDispose` for per-screen state.** Especially for `AsyncNotifier` that fires a network request.
**5. `.family` for parameterized providers.** `userProvider(id)` — one provider spec, many concrete instances.
**6. Adopt `riverpod_generator` (`@riverpod`) for less boilerplate.** Generates the provider declaration from a function or class.
**7. Test with `ProviderContainer` — no widgets required.**
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — CounterNotifier.** Sync int state; increment / reset methods.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — AsyncNotifier fetch user.** `userProvider` reads a repository; UI uses `.when` for loading/data/error.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — `.family` per id.** `userProvider(id)` — verify each id caches independently.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Optimistic update.** Toggle 'liked' on a post; if server fails, revert the state.
**Practice 5 (Hard) — `ProviderContainer` test.** Test the `CounterNotifier` without any widget pump.
**Practice 6 (Expert) — `.autoDispose` + `keepAlive` on scroll.** A search-result provider that disposes when the screen leaves but keeps alive briefly to survive tab switches.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is Riverpod and how does it differ from Provider?**

**A:** Riverpod is Provider's spiritual successor by the same author. Same conceptual model — a dependency graph of state that the widget tree can watch — but structurally different: **(1) No `BuildContext`** — providers are top-level `final` variables; consumers use a `ref` object that works everywhere (widgets, tests, controllers, isolates). **(2) Compile-safe** — a missing provider is a build error, not `ProviderNotFoundException` at runtime. **(3) `AsyncValue<T>`** built-in — sealed loading/data/error state, no ad-hoc `bool _loading` flags. **(4) `.autoDispose`** — automatic lifecycle cleanup. **(5) Better testing** — `ProviderContainer` runs state without widgets. Same InheritedWidget-style graph; better ergonomics. In 2026 it's the default for new Flutter apps.

**Q2: What are the main provider families?**

**A:** **`Provider<T>`** — pure DI; provides a value that never changes. **`NotifierProvider<N, T>`** — synchronous mutable state; `Notifier<T>` has a `build()` returning initial state and methods that mutate `state`. **`AsyncNotifierProvider<N, T>`** — async version; `AsyncNotifier<T>` returns `Future<T>` from `build()`, state is exposed as `AsyncValue<T>`. **`FutureProvider<T>`** / **`StreamProvider<T>`** — one-off Future / continuous Stream, both yielding `AsyncValue<T>`. **`.family`** modifier — parameterize a provider so `userProvider(42)` and `userProvider(43)` are separate instances. **`.autoDispose`** modifier — auto-cleanup when no widget watches. Choose based on state shape: sync/async, mutable/immutable, parameterized/not.

**Q3: What is `AsyncValue<T>` and why is it useful?**

**A:** `AsyncValue<T>` is a sealed value in ONE of three states: **`loading`**, **`data(T)`**, or **`error(Object, StackTrace)`**. Instead of managing `bool _loading; String? _error; T? _data;` flags manually (and constantly forgetting an edge case like error-then-loading transitions), you write `state = AsyncValue.loading();` then `state = AsyncValue.data(user);` or `state = AsyncValue.error(e, s);`. UIs consume via `state.when(loading: ..., data: ..., error: ...)` — the compiler forces you to handle every case. **`AsyncValue.guard(() => api.fetch())`** wraps errors automatically. This is Flutter's modern way to model server state.

**Q4: `ref.watch` vs `ref.read` vs `ref.listen`?**

**A:** **`ref.watch(p)`** — reads AND subscribes; the widget rebuilds on change. Use inside `build()`. **`ref.read(p)`** — reads without subscribing. Use in callbacks (`onPressed`). **`ref.read(p.notifier)`** — accesses the underlying `Notifier` so you can call mutation methods. **`ref.listen(p, (prev, next) => ...)`** — fires a callback on change; doesn't rebuild. Perfect for side effects: navigate on auth-state change, show a toast on error. **`ref.invalidate(p)`** — force refresh, useful for pull-to-refresh. **Rule:** watch in build, read in callbacks, listen for side effects. Same discipline as `BlocBuilder` vs `BlocListener`.

**Q5: What is `.family` and when do you use it?**

**A:** `.family` parameterizes a provider: instead of one `userProvider` for a single user, you get `userProvider(int id)` — separate cached instances per id. `ref.watch(userProvider(42))` and `ref.watch(userProvider(43))` are independent providers. Use for **per-id fetches** — user by id, product by SKU, chat by conversation-id. Under the hood, Riverpod hashes the argument and caches results per hash. **Rule:** the argument must be `hashCode`/`equals` friendly — use `int`, `String`, `Freezed` classes; NEVER a mutable `List`. Combine with `.autoDispose` for auto-cleanup when no widget watches that particular instance.

**Q6: What is `.autoDispose` and when do you need it?**

**A:** Without `.autoDispose`, a provider lives for the whole app lifetime — even when no widget uses it. That's fine for singletons (repo, config). For **screen-scoped state** (a search-query provider that's only relevant while the search screen is open), add `.autoDispose`: `NotifierProvider.autoDispose<...>`. When the last widget stops watching, Riverpod disposes the provider. **`ref.keepAlive()`** inside an autoDispose provider prevents disposal even if watchers drop — useful for briefly surviving tab switches. **`ref.onDispose(() => …)`** registers a cleanup callback. Together these give surgical control over provider lifetimes.

**Q8: When would you NOT use Riverpod?**

**A:** **(1) Widget-local ephemeral state** — a form field's text, an expanded/collapsed toggle. Use `setState` — Riverpod is overkill for state that never leaves one widget. **(2) A codebase already fully on Bloc/Provider** — a rewrite is rarely worth it; keep what works. **(3) A team with strong preference for another library** — technology harmony matters more than any framework's superiority. **(4) Genuinely tiny apps** — the setup ceremony is real; a 3-screen app might not need it. **My rule:** default to Riverpod for new apps of any size; use `setState` for widget-local state; keep existing codebases on their existing library. Zealous rewrites cause more bugs than they fix.

## Real-world usage

- **New Flutter apps in 2024+** — Riverpod is the modern default.
- **`riverpod_generator`** — code-gen `@riverpod` for cleaner syntax; increasingly the recommended style.
- **Firebase apps** — natural pairing with `StreamProvider`/`AsyncNotifierProvider`.
- **Multi-module apps** — providers can be scoped and overridden per feature.
- **Testing** — `ProviderContainer` for state tests; `ProviderScope` overrides for widget tests.
- **Migration from Provider** — typically 1:1: `ChangeNotifierProvider` → `NotifierProvider`; `StreamProvider` → `StreamProvider`; `Provider.of(context)` → `ref.watch/read`.

## Advanced notes

- **`@riverpod` code-gen (`riverpod_generator`)** — generates the provider from a function or class.
- **`ref.onDispose(() => …)`** — cleanup hook when the provider is disposed.
- **`ref.invalidate(p)` vs `ref.refresh(p)`** — invalidate marks stale; refresh reads immediately.
- **`ProviderObserver`** — global observer for logging every provider add/update/dispose.
- **`AsyncValue.guard(() => …)`** — wraps a Future in AsyncValue automatically.
- **`StateNotifier` (legacy)** — old API; new code uses `Notifier` and `AsyncNotifier`.
- **Scoping** — nested `ProviderScope` for feature-level providers.
- **`ref.listenSelf`** — a Notifier listens to itself.
- **Circular dependencies** — Riverpod throws at build time — no runtime infinite loops.
- **`Ref` in a Notifier** — persists as long as the notifier is mounted; use in async methods for other provider access.

flashcards.back: No BuildContext, compile-safe, AsyncValue built-in, autoDispose, easy tests.
Sealed: loading | data(T) | error. UI consumes via .when(loading: ..., data: ..., error: ...).
watch = subscribe (in build). read = one-shot (in callbacks). listen = fire callback on change (side effects).
Parameterize a provider — userProvider(id) caches per argument.
Provider auto-disposed when no widget watches; use for screen-scoped state.
ProviderContainer — read/mutate providers without any widget tree.
